name: Scales of Maat
description: The Scales of Maat are a central artifact in Egyptian mythology, representing cosmic balance, truth, and justice. These scales are used in the Hall of Two Truths during the Weighing of the Heart ceremony in the afterlife. Anubis, the jackal-headed god of embalming and the dead, oversees the weighing. On one side of the scale is placed the heart of the deceased, representing their life and actions. On the other side is placed the feather of Maat, representing truth, justice, and cosmic order. If the heart is lighter than or equal to the feather, the deceased is deemed worthy and allowed to pass into the afterlife to be with Osiris. However, if the heart is heavier than the feather, weighed down by sin and evil deeds, it is devoured by Ammit, the Devourer of Souls, a creature with the head of a crocodile, the forequarters of a lion, and the hindquarters of a hippopotamus, resulting in eternal oblivion. The Scales of Maat are not merely a physical object but a powerful symbol of moral responsibility and the consequences of one's actions in life. They underscore the importance of living a life aligned with the principles of Maat, emphasizing truth, order, and righteousness. The concept embodies the Egyptian belief in a just and ordered universe, where actions have consequences and the soul is ultimately judged based on its adherence to these principles.
subtitle: Balance, Truth, and Justice in Afterlife
origin: Created by the gods to maintain cosmic order
currentLocation: Hall of Two Truths in the Duat (Underworld)

keyMyths.title: The Weighing of the Heart
keyMyths.description: The Scales of Maat are central to the Weighing of the Heart ceremony. If the heart is heavier than the feather of Maat, the deceased is condemned.

keyMyths.title: Maat's Role
keyMyths.description: Maat, the goddess of truth and justice, is symbolized by the feather placed on the scales. Her presence ensures the fairness of the judgment.

sources.author: E.A. Wallis Budge
sources.work: The Egyptian Book of the Dead
sources.description: Translation and analysis of the Book of the Dead, which details the Weighing of the Heart ceremony.
sources.text: Translation and analysis of the Book of the Dead, which details the Weighing of the Heart ceremony.

sources.author: James P. Allen
sources.work: Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs
sources.description: Provides cultural context and linguistic information relating to ancient Egyptian beliefs.
sources.text: Provides cultural context and linguistic information relating to ancient Egyptian beliefs.

worshipPractices.name: Moral Living
worshipPractices.description: Devotees sought to live a life aligned with Ma'at, embodying truth, justice, and cosmic balance. This involved upholding moral principles, acting with integrity, and striving for harmony in their daily lives. By doing so, they hoped to ensure a favorable judgment in the afterlife.

worshipPractices.name: Temple Offerings
worshipPractices.description: While the Scales of Maat themselves weren't directly worshipped, offerings were made to deities associated with justice and truth, such as Ma'at herself. These offerings, often presented in temples, were intended to maintain cosmic order and seek divine favor for a positive afterlife outcome.

festivals.name: Festival of Ma'at
festivals.timing: Various points throughout the year, especially during judicial proceedings or royal coronations.
festivals.description: This festival celebrated the principles of truth, justice, and cosmic order. It often involved processions, rituals, and declarations emphasizing the importance of living in accordance with Ma'at to ensure stability and harmony in the world.

festivals.name: Weighing of the Heart Reenactments
festivals.description: While not a widespread public festival, elements of the Weighing of the Heart ceremony were sometimes reenacted during funerary rites. These performances served to remind the living and the deceased of the importance of a virtuous life and the consequences of a heart burdened with sin.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Scales of Maat remain a powerful symbol of justice, truth, and ethical balance. Their image and concept are frequently used in modern art, literature, and legal systems to represent fairness and the importance of moral responsibility. They also influence philosophical discussions on ethics and the consequences of one's actions.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Zeus uses golden scales to weigh the fates of men, particularly in battle, determining who will live or die. This is similar to the Scales of Maat judging the worthiness of a soul based on the weight of their heart.
While not scales, the Gjallarhorn's sounding heralds Ragnarok and the final judgment, similar to how the Scales of Maat represent a final reckoning. Both signify a moment of truth and consequence.

Chitragupta maintains records of human deeds, which are used by Yama, the god of death, to judge souls in the afterlife. This parallels the function of the Scales of Maat, where actions are assessed to determine a soul's fate.
The Book of Life contains the names of those destined for salvation, determining who enters heaven. Like the Scales of Maat, it represents a divine judgment based on actions and worthiness.

associations.name: Justice
associations.significance: The Scales directly represent the dispensing of justice and judgment in the afterlife.

associations.name: Balance
associations.significance: The scales symbolize the equilibrium between truth and falsehood, order and chaos.

associations.name: Gold
associations.significance: Gold, a precious metal, represents the divine and eternal nature of truth being weighed.

associations.name: Jackal
associations.significance: Associated with Anubis, the jackal connects the scales to funerary rites and the passage to the afterlife.

associations.name: Stars
associations.significance: The stars represent the cosmic order and the unchanging truth that the Scales embody.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: White signifies purity and truth, reflecting the desired outcome of the heart's weighing.

associations.name: Two
associations.significance: Reflects the dual nature of the weighing, balancing the heart against Maat.

companions.name: Anubis
companions.description: Anubis, the jackal-headed god, oversees the Weighing of the Heart ceremony where the Scales of Maat are used, ensuring the process is conducted fairly. He guides the deceased to the scales and presents their heart for judgement.

companions.name: Thoth
companions.description: Thoth, the god of wisdom and writing, records the results of the Weighing of the Heart ceremony as determined by the Scales of Maat. He ensures the accurate transcription of whether the deceased is worthy of entering the afterlife.

companions.name: Ammit
companions.description: Ammit, the Devourer of Souls, stands ready to consume the hearts of those deemed unworthy by the Scales of Maat. Her presence reinforces the importance of living a life of balance and truth.

companions.name: Maat
companions.description: Maat is the goddess of truth, justice, and cosmic order, and the Scales are a physical representation of her principles. As the embodiment of balance, her essence is intrinsically linked to the Scales.

extendedContent.title: Creation & Forging in the Primordial Void
extendedContent.content:
The Scales of Maat are not typically described as being 'forged' in the same way as weapons or tools. Instead, their existence is intrinsically linked to the very creation of the cosmos and the establishment of Maat, the principle of cosmic order and truth. The concept of Maat predates the gods themselves, existing as the fundamental principle that allowed Ra to emerge from the chaotic waters of Nun and begin the process of creation. Therefore, the Scales of Maat are seen less as a crafted object and more as a manifestation of this primordial Maat. 

Some texts suggest that the Scales emerged alongside Maat, perhaps visualized as being woven from the very fabric of reality. The act of weighing itself can be considered an ongoing process of 'forging' and reaffirming the balance that sustains the universe. The presence of Thoth, the scribe god, recording the results of the weighing, further emphasizes the cosmic importance of this event. The scales are not merely an instrument of judgment; they are a crucial component in the ongoing maintenance of cosmic harmony. 

While specific textual references detailing the physical creation of the Scales are scarce, the Pyramid Texts, particularly those relating to the solar cult, hint at the importance of balance and order in the creation cycle. The imagery of the rising sun and the daily journey of Ra across the sky reinforces the idea of a divinely ordained equilibrium, which the Scales of Maat symbolize and uphold.

extendedContent.title: Powers & Properties: Reflecting Cosmic Balance
extendedContent.content:
The primary power of the Scales of Maat lies in their ability to accurately measure the weight of the heart, a symbolic representation of a person's life and deeds. This weight is compared against the feather of Maat (a single ostrich feather, or sometimes a miniature representation of the goddess herself), representing truth, justice, and cosmic order. The Scales are not merely a physical device, but a conduit for divine judgment. If the heart is lighter than the feather, the deceased is deemed worthy of entering the afterlife and continuing their journey in the Field of Reeds (Aaru). 

If, however, the heart is heavier than the feather, weighed down by sin, deceit, and wrongdoing, the deceased is condemned to utter annihilation. Their heart is devoured by Ammit, the fearsome hybrid creature with the head of a crocodile, the body of a lion, and the hindquarters of a hippopotamus. This is the 'second death', a permanent cessation of existence. The Scales, therefore, possess the power to grant eternal life or condemn to eternal nothingness. 

Beyond the individual judgment, the Scales of Maat also symbolize the broader concept of cosmic balance. They represent the need for individuals and society as a whole to maintain harmony and uphold Maat. Dishonesty, injustice, and chaos upset this balance and ultimately threaten the stability of the world. The Scales serve as a constant reminder of the consequences of disrupting the divinely ordained order and the importance of living a life guided by truth and righteousness.

extendedContent.title: Notable Wielders and Divine Associations
extendedContent.content:
While the Scales of Maat are not actively 'wielded' in the traditional sense, several deities are intrinsically linked to their function and significance. Anubis, the jackal-headed god of embalming and the dead, is the primary figure responsible for overseeing the Weighing of the Heart ceremony. He carefully places the heart and the feather on the Scales and ensures the accuracy of the measurement. Anubis acts as the guardian and guide of the deceased through the perilous journey of the afterlife. 

Thoth, the ibis-headed god of wisdom, writing, and knowledge, plays a crucial role as the scribe of the gods. He meticulously records the results of the weighing, ensuring that the judgment is properly documented and carried out. Thoth's presence emphasizes the importance of truth and accuracy in the process. Maat, the goddess of truth, justice, and cosmic order, is herself inseparable from the Scales. Her feather serves as the standard against which the heart is measured, representing the ideal that all individuals should strive to achieve. 

Osiris, the god of the afterlife and resurrection, presides over the Hall of Two Truths, where the Weighing of the Heart takes place. He is the ultimate judge and the gatekeeper to the afterlife. While not directly involved in the weighing process, Osiris's presence underscores the gravity and importance of the ceremony. The Scales of Maat, therefore, are not associated with a single wielder, but rather function as part of a divine ensemble working to maintain cosmic order and ensure justice in the afterlife.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism: Truth, Justice, and the Afterlife
extendedContent.content:
The Scales of Maat are a potent symbol of several core concepts in Egyptian mythology and religious belief. At their most fundamental, they represent the importance of truth and justice in both earthly life and the afterlife. The act of weighing the heart against the feather of Maat symbolizes the evaluation of a person's moral character and the consequences of their actions. The Scales serve as a reminder that all deeds, both good and bad, are ultimately accounted for and have a lasting impact on one's fate. 

The Scales also represent the delicate balance that exists between order and chaos. Maat, as the principle of cosmic order, is constantly threatened by the forces of chaos and disruption. The Weighing of the Heart ceremony can be seen as a symbolic reassertion of Maat, ensuring that those who have lived in accordance with her principles are rewarded, while those who have disrupted the balance are punished. This reinforces the importance of maintaining harmony and upholding ethical conduct in all aspects of life. 

Furthermore, the Scales are a powerful symbol of hope and redemption. The possibility of passing the test and entering the afterlife offers solace and reassurance to the living. It underscores the belief that even in the face of death, there is the potential for eternal life and happiness, provided one has lived a life guided by truth and righteousness. The Scales of Maat, therefore, are not just an instrument of judgment, but also a symbol of the enduring power of Maat and the promise of a just and harmonious afterlife.

shortDescription: The Scales of Maat embody cosmic balance in Egyptian afterlife beliefs. Used in the Weighing of the Heart ceremony, they determine a soul's worthiness.

wielders.name: Anubis
wielders.context: Oversees the Weighing of the Heart ceremony.

wielders.name: Maat
wielders.context: The goddess of truth and justice, representing cosmic order.

wielders.name: Thoth
wielders.context: Records the results of the weighing of the heart.
